coredata.dc:description: Much attention has been drawn to develop efficient poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) recycling methods. Reported here is the solar-driven PET recycling coupled with H2 production with carbonized polymer dots-graphitic carbon nitride (CPDs-CN) as catalyst. PET plastic is a “greener” alternative feedstock where its hydrolytic monomer ethylene glycol is converted into high added-value chemicals, mainly including glycolic acid, glycolaldehyde and ethanol; meanwhile, H2 is produced from water splitting. The monomer terephthalic acid yield reaches 304.7 ± 17.2 μmol, exceeding the value of the catalyst-free group nearly twofold. Increased H2 production rate upon hybridization with CPDs is achieved (1034 ± 134 vs. 291 ± 35 μmol g–1 h–1, respectively). The adopted strategy allows plastic waste to be used as a valuable feedstock for the sustainable production of value-added solar chemicals and solar fuels.

White lines denote the (100) intralayer spacing. d) XRD patterns of g-C3N4 and CPDs-CN. e) Physical colors extracted from the digital photographs of g-C3N4 and CPDs-CN. f) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of GCN and CPDs-CN.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Much attention has been drawn to develop efficient poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) recycling methods. Reported here is the solar-driven PET recycling coupled with H2 production with carbonized polymer dots-graphitic carbon nitride (CPDs-CN) as catalyst. PET plastic is a “greener” alternative feedstock where its hydrolytic monomer ethylene glycol is converted into high added-value chemicals, mainly including glycolic acid, glycolaldehyde and ethanol; meanwhile, H2 is produced from water splitting. The monomer terephthalic acid yield reaches 304.7 ± 17.2 μmol, exceeding the value of the catalyst-free group nearly twofold. Increased H2 production rate upon hybridization with CPDs is achieved (1034 ± 134 vs. 291 ± 35 μmol g–1 h–1, respectively). The adopted strategy allows plastic waste to be used as a valuable feedstock for the sustainable production of value-added solar chemicals and solar fuels.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Plastics have a wide application scope due to their numerous advantages such as resistance to corrosion and cracking, low density, and versatility. However, solid plastic waste (SPW) swamps landfills and harms the marine and freshwater ecosystems, thus posing a global threat to living organisms [1]. In view of its chemical structure, most SPW, resists natural degradation [2] and typically requires hundreds of years for complete decomposition, which makes its end-of-life management a significant issue. Despite their abundance, the available SPW management strategies, which are mainly based on landfilling, incineration, and recycling, suffer from the low degradation activity and produce microplastics (small plastic particles with sizes of <5 mm), which results in secondary pollution [3,4]. The catalytic deconstruction of various plastic feedstocks into hydrogen and high-value carbons is an attractive topic. Jie et al. realized the rapid extraction of ~97 % of H2 from plastic waste using a simple one-step microwave-initiated process promoted by a highly active FeAlO x catalyst [5]. Additionally, plastics can also be photodegraded into CH3COOH through sequential photoinduced C–C bond cleavage and coupling over Nb2O5 atomic layers [6,7].

As the most abundant polyester plastic, poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) is a major contributor to SPW [8]. Although PET hydrolysis can be accelerated by treatment with hydrolytic enzymes [9,10], alkaline hydrolysis is often preferentially used to avoid the restrictions of enzymatic hydrolysis and depolymerize polyester plastics into the corresponding monomers. Alkaline hydrolysis is a first-order process with respect to the concentration of hydroxide ions, and its activation energy in water is lower than that of neutral solid-state heterogeneous hydrolysis [11]. The chemical recycling of post-consumer SPW is an excellent method of hindering the explosive growth of marine and land pollution, mitigating disposal problems, and incorporating enabling the incorporation of the circular economy principle in today’s society [12]. Zhou et al. demonstrated an electrocatalytic upcycling strategy for PET waste to produce valuable H2, terephthalic acid (TPA), andpotassium diformate [13]. Photocatalytic technology has been recognized as one of the promising proposals for environmental remediation and energy conversion. Recently, a novel 2D in-plane NiO/Fe2O3 heterostructure was proved to accelerate the carrier dynamics for achieving improved PET photoconversion into CO2 efficiency [14]. Furthermore, solar-driven H2 production via water splitting using plastic waste presents a novel, sustainable and environmentally friendly plastics recycling method relying on the quenching of photoinduced holes (h+) and the resulting accumulation of low-potential electrons (e−) for the reduction of H+ to H2 [15,16]. Despite its immaturity, the coupling of H2 production with the conversion of organics to value-added chemicals has drawn much attention and has significantly advanced over the past few years [17–20]. However, low-cost and environmentally benign photocatalysts with sufficiently high activity and stability remain highly sought after.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials with promising biomedical and optoelectronic applications[21,22] and are subdivided into graphene quantum dots [23,24], carbon nanodots [25], carbon quantum dots [26], and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs)[27,28] based on their synthesis, structure, and properties. Despite their diversity, CDs can harness a broad range of visible or near-infrared light[29,30] and are capable of photoinduced electron transfer and storage [31–33], thus having the capacity to enhance the performance of main photocatalysts. Moreover, special “core-shell” nanostructures endow CPDs with high stability/compatibility and an ability to induce the formation of more active sites [34,35].

In view of its appropriate electronic band structure, high chemical stability, and upscalable synthesis, metal-free graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a promising next-generation light absorber for photocatalytic water splitting [36–39]. Generally, g-C3N4 can be synthesized via the polycondensation of C- and N-containing organics such as urea, melamine, and dicyandiamide, and its performance can be enhanced using strategies such as heteroatom doping [40], N vacancy introduction [41], hybridization [42–45], and nanostructure engineering [46,47]. The advantages of g-C3N4, namely its suitable structure and ultrahigh stability, make it an ideal host for the robust accommodation of ultrasmall CPDs.

Herein, we report the design and synthesis of carbonized polymer dots-graphitic carbon nitride (CPDs-CN) composites as binary catalysts for the upcycling of PET involving photocatalytic H2 production. As depicted in Scheme 1, this process affords EG along with the photocatalytic value-added products (mainly glycolic acid, glycolaldehyde, and ethanol), high-purity terephthalic acid (TPA), and H2 fuels. In the presence of CPDs-CN catalyst, the TPA yield reached 304.7 ± 17.2 μmol after 22-h hydrolysis, exceeding the value obtained for the control group nearly two-fold. Upon hybridization with CPDs, high H2 production rate of 1034 ± 134 μmol g-1 h-1 was observed. Ultimately, this work provides a design strategy to incentivize PET reclamation and reduce plastic pollution, which advances many global sustainability goals by combining H2 fuels with reclaimed plastics.

Citric acid (CA), terephthalic acid, isophthalic acid, L-(+)-lactic acid, ethylene glycol, alcohol, glycolaldehyde dimer, formic acid, acetic acid, and TiO2 (anatase phase) were purchased from Aladdin. Ethylenediamine (EDA), NaOD (40 wt% in D2O), and sodium terephthalate were purchased from Alfa Aesar. D2O (99.96 atom% D), polyethylene terephthalate (PET, white granular, 3 mm, viscosity 0.8–0.83 mPa·s), polylactic acid (PLA, white granular, 3 mm, Mw 80,000), and acetaldehyde were obtained from Shanghai Macklin. KOD (40 wt% in D2O), and maleic acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. KOH and Na2SO4 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Regent. Chloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6.6 H2O) was obtained from Energy Chemical. Deionized (DI) water (> 18 MΩ·cm) were generated via a Milli-Q system. Other organic solvents were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works. All reagents were used as received without further purification.

CPDs was synthesized via a modified method according to previous work [48]. Typically, 1.0 mmol of CA and 1.0 mmol of EDA were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. Then, the solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 200ºC for 6 h. After the reaction, the reactors were cooled to room temperature naturally and subjected to dialysis with molecular weight cut off 500–1000. The dry products were obtained by freeze-drying.

The synthetic procedure was based on previous work [49]. Subsequently, 100 mg of obtained powder was dispersed in 10 mL deionized water via sonication for 30 min at room temperature. Thereafter, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 180ºC for 6 h. After the reaction, the reactors were cooled to room temperature naturally. The product was centrifuged and washed with water for three times and dried at 60ºC oven to obtain light yellow GCN nanosheets.

Typically, 100 mg of product powder was added to x mL (x = 1, 3, 5, 7 or 10) of CPDs solution (1 mg mL–1) and water was added to a total volume of 10 mL. Subsequently, the treatment of the suspension was analogous to the synthesis of g-C3N4 and the final products were obtained.

A specific quantity of commercial plastic was added into KOH (5 M, unless otherwise stated) aqueous solution in a round-bottom flask via heating reflux to initiate the hydrolysis. In order to prevent bumping, the mixture was under continuous magnetic stirring. PLA was heated at 100ºC for 2 h, while PET at 120ºC for 24 h. As for commercial PLA packaging bags and PET drink bottles, they were cut into small pieces, washed and dried to clean surface impurities prior to heating reflux.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were performed on a JEM-2100F at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was performed in the tapping mode with a Nanoscope IIIa scanning probe microscope from Digital Instruments under ambient conditions. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu 3100 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The UV–vis DRS spectra were acquired on a UV/VIS/NIR spectrophotometer (Lambda 950, Perkinelmer) with white standard of BaSO4 powder used as the reference. And the absorption spectra were calculated from the reflectance data with the Kubelka-Munk function. The crystal phases of the samples were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) operating at 40 kV and 40 mA with a fixed slit (PANalytical B.V. Empyrean). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed on a Brucker FTIR spectrophotometer (VERTEX-80 V) using KBr pallets at room temperature. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were performed with AS 400 (Q.One Instruments Ltd., 400 MHz) and a Bruker AVANCE NMR spectrometer (600 MHz). In order to quantitatively analyze the yield of products in aqueous solution, 10 vol% D2O was added, and the solvent suppression method was adopted using maleic acid (δ = 5.73 ppm, 50 mg mL–1 in D2O) used as internal standard. PL lifetimes were collected on FLS 920 (Edinburgh Instrument). The compositions and chemical states of the samples were analyzed using X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS) Microprobe, conducted on Thermo ESCALAB-250Xi spectrometer with a mono X-ray source with Al Kα excitation.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), transient photocurrent response measurements and Mott-Schottky plots were carried out in a conventional three electrode cell assembly using tested by a CHI 660 workstation (CH Instruments, Chenhua, Shanghai, China). The working electrodes were conducted as follows: 2 mg mL–1 of the photocatalyst dispersed in ethanol was spin-coated onto FTO glass (1 × 1 cm2). After air-drying for 2 h, the working electrode was further dried in the oven at 60 °C to improve adhesion. Pt-electrode was employed as counter electrode. Ag/AgCl electrode was used as reference electrode. EIS experiments were performed within a frequency range from 10-1 Hz to 2 × 105 Hz. The photocurrent-time dependence was measured under chopped irradiation with 30 s light on/off cycles. The electrolyte was 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution in the experiments.

The PET hydrolytic activity was assessed by a multichannel photochemical reaction system (PCX-50 C, Beijing Perfectlight Co., Ltd, China). In a typical reaction, 10 mg of the catalyst was dispersed by ultrasonication for 30 min in a quartz bottle containing 50 mL of 1 M KOH aqueous solution. Then, 50 mg of PET powder was added. Pt-cocatalyst was synthesized via in situ photo-deposition by adding a unique amount of H2PtCl6.6 H2O aqueous solution as a precursor. To monitor the production yield of TPA by PET hydrolysis, 2.0 mL samples were filtered to remove the particles with a 0.22 µm Millipore filter following a certain time interval. TPA was precipitated by adding hydrochloric acid and quantitatively analyzed using 1H NMR spectra.

The photocatalytic tests were carried out in a sealed gas circulation and evacuation system using a 300 W xenon lamp (CEL-HXF300, Beijing Ceaulight Technology Co., Ltd., China) equipped with an AM 1.5 G filter as light source. In a typical test, 20 mg of catalyst and 1.25 g of PET powder were dispersed in 50 mL of 1 M KOH aqueous solution in a reaction cell by sonication. Prior to illumination, the reactor is deaerated by evacuation to assure vacuum environment with stirring for 20 min and then irradiated from the top of the reaction cell through a quartz window. Pt-cocatalyst was synthesized via in situ photo-deposition by adding a unique amount of H2PtCl6.6 H2O aqueous solution as a precursor. During irradiation, the temperature was maintained at 40 °C by oil bath and the samples were stirred constantly for 22 h. TPA was precipitated by adding hydrochloric acid and quantitatively analyzed using 1H NMR spectra. The amount of generated H2 was analyzed using an online gas chromatograph (GC-7920, N2 as carrier gas, thermal conductivity detection) every half an hour. For the pre-treated PET plastic, the temperature was maintained at 6 °C by a flow of cooling water. Other conditions were the same as above unless otherwise stated.

The ROS detection was carried out with the sodium terephthalate fluorescent probe. Prior to illumination, 1 mM sodium terephthalate was added to a 50 mL suspension containing 20 mg of catalyst and 10 vol% TEOA in water. The fluorescence was measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu RF5301 PC) with excited wavelength of 310 nm.

CPDs were prepared from citric acid and ethylenediamine. The UV–vis spectrum of CPDs (Fig. S1) featured peaks centered at 240 and 340 nm, which were assigned to the π→π * transitions in conjugated aromatic rings with sp2 carbons and the n→π * transitions of the lone-pair electrons in CO moieties, respectively [50]. Fig. 1a shows a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of CPDs, revealing that their diameter ranged from 1.3 to 4.1 nm (average = 2.7 nm). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image revealed a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) lattice plane of graphitic carbon. Height profiles determined using atomic force microscopy (Fig. S2) revealed a height of 1.3–7.0 nm. The broad X-ray diffraction (XRD) peak at ~25° confirmed the presence of a graphitic structure (Fig. S3) [51]. Further information was extracted using 1H and 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. The 1H NMR spectrum of CPDs (Fig. S4a) featured signals at 5.6–6.0 ppm, assigned to protons bound to sp2 carbons [52]. The corresponding 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. S4b) revealed the presence of CO bonds (surface; δ = 170–180 ppm) [53], sp2 carbons (graphitic, amorphous; δ = 140–160 ppm), and sp3 carbons (amorphous; δ = 25–50 ppm) [54–56]. Moreover, three exchangeable protons (δ = 8.4, 7.4, and 4.4 ppm) were observed in DMSO-d 6 (Fig. S5). Thus, CPDs most likely had a polycyclic aromatic core featuring a large conjugated graphitic sp2 carbon hybrid with sp3 carbons [57].

The functional groups of CPDs were further probed by Fourier transfer infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The FTIR spectrum of CPDs (Fig. S6) featured peaks corresponding to the stretching of O–H/N–H (3131 cm−1) and C–H (3022 cm−1) bonds as well as those corresponding to the bending of N–H bonds (1576 cm−1). The peaks at 1668, 1396, and 1179 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching of CO, C–N, and C–OH bonds, respectively. The survey X-ray photoelectron spectrum of CPDs indicated the exclusive presence of C, N, and O (Fig. S7 and Tables S1–S3). The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum could be deconvoluted into the contributions of C–C/CC, C–N, C–OH, and CO moieties. The N 1 s spectrum revealed the presence of three types of N atoms, namely graphitic N, amide N, and amine N, while the O 1 s spectrum was deconvoluted into the contributions of N–CO, O–CO, and C–OH groups [58]. Together, the above results suggest the presence of carboxylic, hydroxyl, and amino groups in CPDs.

TEM imaging showed that GCN featured a flat lamellar structure (Fig. S8), while its composites featured CPDs homogeneously dispersed on the g-C3N4 surface, with the lattice spacing of GCN equalling 0.21 nm (Fig. 1b, Figs. S9 and S10). The average sizes of CPDs in CPDs-CN-3, CPDs-CN-5, and CPDs-CN-7 were estimated as 1.9, 1.9, and 2.4 nm, respectively. On the one hand, the surface functional groups of CPDs engaged in chemical bonding with GCN to decrease CPDs size. On the other hand, the low contrast between the polymer chains of CPDs and GCN complicated the observation of individual composite components. Elemental mapping showed that the constituent elements of the composites were well distributed (Fig. S11). Actual CPDs loadings were determined by probing the concentration of the solution after hydrothermal treatment via UV–vis spectroscopy at a specific wavelength of 420 nm (Fig. S12 and Table S4).

The XRD patterns of GCN and CPDs-CN (Fig. 1d) featured peaks at 13.2° and 27.5°, which were attributed to the (100) crystal plane of in-plane repeating tri-s-thiazine units and the (002) crystal plane of stacked conjugated aromatic systems, respectively. The peak of g-C3N4 at 13.2° did not disappear upon hybridization with CPDs, i.e., their introduction did not destroy the periodic potential field of the oriented melons. The N2 adsorption-desorption tests also confirmed this result as shown in Fig. S13. The samples exhibit type IV isotherms and the specific surface area of GCN and CPDs-CN-7 were 43.1 and 49.8 m2 g−1, respectively. The corresponding FTIR spectra (Fig. S14) featured a sharp peak at 810 cm−1, which was ascribed to the out-of-plane bending of heptazine rings, and peaks at 3100–3500 and 1200–1700 cm−1, which were ascribed to the stretching of N–H bonds and N–CN heterorings in the melon framework, respectively [49]. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of GCN (Fig. S15a, b and Table S1) was deconvoluted into three Gaussian-Lorentzian peaks at 287.3, 284.5 and 283.8 eV corresponding to N–CN coordination in the g-C3N4 framework, C–NH x (x = 1, 2) moieties on the edges of heptazine units, and graphitic C–C/CC groups, respectively [41]. In the N 1 s spectra, the small peak at 404.0 eV was attributed to positive charge localization in heterocycles, while the prominent peak at 398.5 eV was deconvoluted into three signals at 398.3, 398.9, and 400.2 eV corresponding to sp2 N atoms in triazine rings (C–NC), bridging N atoms in N–(C)3 and amino groups (C–NH) due to imperfect polymerization (Fig. S15c and Table S2) [46]. The deconvoluted O 1 s peak at 532.5 eV was ascribed to adsorbed water (Oads) [58], while the peak at 531.2 eV was attributed to hydroxyl groups (C–OH) (Fig. S15d and Table S3). To confirm the successful hybridization of g-C3N4 with CPDs, we compared the X-ray photoelectron spectra of CPDs-CN with those of their constituents. Unlike that of g-C3N4, the C 1 s spectra of CPDs-CN could be deconvoluted into four peaks. The C 1 s peak at 287.1 or 287.7 eV was assigned to CO groups (Fig. S16a, b and Table S1), while the N 1 s peak of CPDs-CN-3 at 399.5 eV (Fig. S16c and Table S2) was assigned to amide groups (N–CO). Furthermore, the O 1s spectra of CPDs-CN-3 were deconvoluted into the signals of N–CO, C–OH and Oads, featuring no contribution of O–CO moieties (Fig. S27d and Table S3). These results revealed that CPDs were indeed incorporated into GCN via amide bond formation, which resulted in the consumption of carboxyl groups [59]. The presence of carboxyl groups in CPDs-CN-7 (Fig. S17) was mainly ascribed to their incomplete reaction with the amine groups of g-C3N4. It is worth noting that hybridization with CPDs resulted in a color change from light yellow to dark brown (Fig. 1e) and thus efficiently extended the light absorption of GCN in the entire visible region (as indicated by intense absorption at 400–800 nm, Fig. 1f).

Alkaline hydrolysis is commonly used to depolymerize polyester plastics into monomers completely. The alkaline hydrolysis of PET afforded EG, TPA, and isophthalic acid (IPA) (Fig. S18). Numerous other chemicals were subsequently produced during the photocatalytic process. To shed light on the formation of EG-derived chemicals, we pre-treated PET plastic in an alkaline solution and analyzed the types and yields of the obtained products using 1H and 13C NMR spectroscopy. First, the NMR spectra were compared with those of authentic molecules to verify product identities (Figs. S19–S21). Maleic acid was used as an internal standard for quantitative analysis. The photogenerated species consisted of glycolaldehyde, glycolic acid, formic acid, ethanol, acetaldehyde and acetic acid, with the respective yields determined as 110, 383, 139, 128, 43, and 554 μmol after eight days of irradiation over CPDs-CN-7 catalyst ( Figs. 2a,b, S21 and S22). We define intermediate selectivity based on the ratio between the mole of one specific product and the total products. The significant findings included: (1) the PET plastic conversion increased monotonically as a function of time; (2) glycollic acid and acetic acid afforded high selectivity (Fig. 2c); (3) there was few variation of intermediate selectivity towards other products including glycolaldehyde, formic acid, ethanol and acetaldehyde.

To clarify the reaction pathway, we intentionally added different kinds of liquid products into the reaction systems, respectively, which is a direct strategy for identifying photogenerated products since the added chemicals can be consumed and converted under light irradiation. In this study, with the addition of glycolaldehyde to the reaction system, higher yields of glycollic acid, formic acid, acetaldehyde and acetic acid could be achieved compared to the control experiment (Fig. 2d, Table 1). The additional glycolic acid was converted to formic acid. By adding ethanol into the reaction system, the yields of acetaldehyde and acetic acid increased; while acetaldehyde was oxidized to acetic acid. The results strongly demonstrated the proposed formation mechanism of several EG derivatives, as shown in Fig. 3a. In the oxidation pathway, EG was first oxidized by the photogenerated holes to glycolaldehyde, which was further oxidized to glycollic acid and formic acid [60]. In addition, EG can also undergo hydrodeoxygenation to produce ethanol [61,62], which was further oxidized to acetaldehyde and acetic acid. Having established a good performance and stability of CPDs-CN-7 composite for photocatalytic upcycling of PET plastic, we aimed to further verify the feasibility of the method. GCN and CPDs-CN-3 photocatalysts were performed for PET transformation. Comparing the product profiles, variable intermediate product distributions were demonstrated (Fig. 3b). Tunable CPDs contents and catalytic capability diversified reaction products, thereby potentially providing alternative routes for controlling catalytic conversion and selectivity on CPDs-CN photocatalysts (Fig. 3c). By contrast, experiments using traditional semiconductor Anatase TiO2 were conducted as catalyst for the above PET conversion process. TiO2 showed lower levels of chemicals production (Fig. 3b) and led to 30 %, 28 %, 6 %, 5 % and 31 % intermediate selectivity toward glycollic acid, formic acid, ethanol, acetaldehyde and acetic acid, respectively (Fig. 3c). Thus, from the viewpoint of the reaction process, photocatalysis-assisted upcycling of PET plastic made the main contribution to the production of value-added EG-derived chemicals. The advances on photocatalysis have enabled development in plastic transformation to generate high-value chemicals.

The produced aromatic moieties were not readily oxidized, offering the basis to calculate product yields in catalytic reactions [15,16]. Herein, PET hydrolysis activity was evaluated in terms of TPA yield after a prolonged reaction time. TPA could be precipitated by acidification with hydrochloric acid without further purification in almost quantitative yield. In the absence of catalyst, this yield equalled 33.7 ± 5.0 μmol after 96-h hydrolysis at ambient temperature under LED illumination, while a higher value of 51.3 ± 1.4, 55.6 ± 6.1 and 32.0 ± 5.5 μmol was observed in the presence of GCN, CPDs-CN-3 and CPDs-CN-10 catalysts ( Fig. 4a, Table 2). At the 0.05 level, the population means are significantly different. Comparison with the NMR spectrum of the TPA standard revealed that this crude TPA was highly pure, featuring residual solvent as the only detectable impurity (Fig. 4b), and could therefore be reused for PET synthesis or in the textile industry to realize the chemical recycling of plastic waste. Furthermore, PET hydrolysis was conducted and maintained at 40ºC under the irradiation of a 300 W Xe lamp. After 22-h alkaline hydrolysis, TPA yields of 157.5 ± 33.2 and 304.7 ± 17.2 μmol were obtained in the absence of catalyst and the presence of CPDs-CN-7, respectively (Fig. 4c, Table 3). In the case of PET film, TPA yields of 127.3 ± 15.1 and 201.1 ± 19.2 μmol after 22-h irradiation were obtained in the absence of catalysts and the presence of CPDs-CN-7, respectively. This technology hence displays promising efficiency as well as advantageous versatility and scalability for utilizing metal-free photocatalysts to promote chemical recycling of PET wastes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic H2 production coupled with PET hydrolysis
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic H2 generation rate of CPDs-CN catalyst was proved to be significantly higher than that previously reported with g-C3N4-composite systems. The wavelength-dependent apparent quantum yield of the same CPDs-CN-7 catalyst was measured, which achieved 2.5 % at 420 nm and 0.22 % at 500 nm [59]. Under the dark conditions, no H2 was produced, which was proved that irradiation was necessary for H2 production in this process. Simultaneous PET hydrolysis coupled with photocatalytic H2 evolution experiments were conducted. Continuous H2 production (Fig. 4d) revealed that GCN achieved a H2 yield of 109.7 μmol after 22 h (PET powder), whereas CPDs-CN-7 showed significantly higher H2 evolution performances, achieving H2 yields of 269.8 μmol (PET powder) and 148.7 μmol (PET film). In order to optimize the conditions, photocatalytic H2 experiments were carried out using pre-treated PET plastic. Fig. 5a presents the H2 generation rates achieved over CPDs-CN-3 for different initial PET dosages. In the absence of pre-treated PET, no H2 was produced. As the initial amount of PET increased to 1.25 g, the H2 production rate reached 687 ± 89 μmol g−1 h−1 (11 ± 1.5 μmol gsub −1 h−1). However, as the increased content of insoluble components affected light absorption, the H2 production rate decreased to 511 ± 76 μmol g−1 h−1 (4.1 ± 0.6 μmol gsub −1 h−1) when PET loading further increased to 2.50 g. Therefore, the optimal initial amount of PET was determined as 1.25 g. Fig. 5b compares the effect of CPDs loading on H2 generation rate, revealing that the maximal rate was achieved for CPDs-CN-7 (1034 ± 134 μmol g−1 h−1), exceeding that achieved for GCN (291 ± 35 μmol g−1 h−1) nearly 3.6-fold. Although the higher content of CPDs in CPDs-CN-10 would absorb more incident light and suppress the photoexcitation of GCN [63], the activity of this catalyst (677 ± 156 μmol g−1 h−1) still exceeded that of GCN. The optimal photosystem was also investigated under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm, Fig. 5c). Under these conditions, the rate of photocatalytic H2 generation equalled 515 ± 168 μmol g−1 h−1 and significantly exceeded that observed for Anatase TiO2 (55 ± 4 μmol g−1 h−1, AM 1.5 G), while no H2 was generated under visible light (λ > 420 nm). Comparison with previous works revealed the superior performance of CPDs-CN for photocatalytic H2 production (Table S5), showing that the achieved rates were of the same order of magnitude as those reported for CdS/CdO x quantum dots. Therefore, non-toxic CPD-CN catalysts were concluded to be promising alternatives to their toxic metal-containing analogues.

Importantly, CDs are known to act as co-catalytic active sites and/or good electron acceptors/donors [58]. To enable platinum-free conditions, we employed CPDs-CN catalysts on photocatalytic H2 generation in the absence of a Pt co-catalyst (Fig. 5d). The H2 generation rates achieved by CPDs-CN-3 (211 ± 46 μmol g−1 h−1) and CPDs-CN-7 (298 ± 58 μmol g−1 h−1) exceeded the value obtained for GCN 2.2- and 3.1-fold, respectively. The catalytic performance of CPDs was quantified in terms of the CPDs-based turnover frequency for CPDs-CN-7 (TOFCPDs = n H2 (n CPDs)−1 h−1). Based on the actual CPDs loading of CPDs-CN-7 (~5.2 wt%; Table S4) and the estimated molecular weight of CPDs (2000 Da), TOFCPDs was determined as 11.4 ± 2.3 h−1. XRD analysis revealed that the main structure of CPDs-CN-7 did not change significantly after the 48-h photocatalytic reaction (Fig. S23). The catalytic activity of CPDs-CN-7 did not significantly change after three-fold cycling (Fig. 4e), and therefore, longer-term stability tests were performed. Fig. 4f shows that CPDs-CN-3 exhibited stable performance over four days, while CPDs-CN-7 showed stable performance for at least nine days, achieving an H2 yield of 2.8 mmol at a catalyst loading of 15 mg. During the photocatalytic tests, CPDs-CN catalysts showed high stability, and no small molecular substances were generated as impurity [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic H2 production coupled with poly(lactic acid) hydrolysis
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the catalyst performance with increasing feedstock complexity, PLA is an aliphatic polyester derived from lactic acid or its dimer, affording lactic acid as the hydrolysis product and carbonate as the photocatalytic degradation product (Figs. S24–S26). In this case, the liquid-phase products were precipitated by addition of Ba(OH)2 and probed by XRD. The resulting pattern featured strong peaks of BaCO3 (PDF # 05-0378), which indicated that PLA was degraded to CO2 (Fig. S27). Pre-treated PLA plastic presented an optimal H2 evolution rate of 1326 ± 181 μmol g−1 h−1 over CPDs-CN catalysts, which was 2.7 times that achieved for g-C3N4 (488 ± 92 μmol g−1 h−1, Fig. S28a). Under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm), the rate of photocatalytic H2 generation over CPDs-CN-7 equalled 247 ± 38 μmol g−1 h−1. The simple (i.e., that produced without hydrothermal treatment) mixture of g-C3N4 and CPDs (7 wt%) achieved an H2 yield of 497 ± 113 μmol g−1 h−1, which was similar to that observed for GCN. The above results show that CPDs and g-C3N4 can form stable high-performance composites. To confirm the capacity of aqueous KOH for plastic hydrolysis, photocatalytic H2 production was conducted simultaneously with the gradual addition of PLA (Fig. S28b). As a result, 16 g of PLA was added to 50 mL of the alkaline solution to achieve neutrality. Notably, the strongly alkaline residual was removed by the lactic acid produced upon hydrolysis to avoid detrimental effects on the natural environment. Therefore, our approach to PLA utilization coupled with H2 production is time-saving (2 h vs. several months required for natural biodegradation) and cost-effective. Furthermore, one issue associated with the practical applications is the catalyst adaptability to raw plastic feedstock. Common SPW such as commercial PLA packaging bags and PET beverage bottles offered H2 generation rates of 498 ± 98 and 706 ± 122 μmol g−1 h−1, respectively, and were therefore identified as promising substrates for photocatalytic H2 production.

High-performance catalysts should effectively utilize the solar spectrum and enable effective charge separation and the fast migration of photogenerated charge carriers. The introduction of CPDs noticeably extended the light-responsive range of GCN to the entire visible region, with the extension degree depending on CPDs loading (Fig. 1e). Thus, CPDs-CN composites exhibited enhanced visible-light-absorption intensities [64], which suggested that these catalysts can gain more solar energy to induce the absorption of low-energy photons and generate more electron-hole pairs under irradiation [65].

Photocarrier separation and transfer efficiencies are the main factors affecting photocatalytic performance [66]. The transition behaviors of charge carriers in CPDs-CN were supported by photoluminescence (PL) spectra. Time-resolved transient PL decay spectra ( Fig. 6a) and the estimated lifetimes (τ AVE) of emission decay (tri-exponential decay, Table S6) revealed that the lifetimes of all CPDs-CN were shorter than that of GCN. Specifically, the τ AVE of GCN, CPDs-CN-1, CPDs-CN-3, CPDs-CN-7, CPDs-CN-10, and CPDs equalled 12.7, 10.2, 9.5, 5.5, 9.0, and 11.3 ns, respectively. These results disclosed that the incorporation of CPDs helps to decrease carrier lifetime and thus accelerates charge transfer and, hence, facilitates charge separation [42]. The notably decreased emission lifetime of CPDs-CN indicates a non-radiative pathway due to the electronic interaction between CPDs and GCN. The electron-transfer rate constants (k
et (CPDs → GCN) = τ(CPDs-CN)−1 − τ(CPDs)−1)[67] of CPDs-CN-1, CPDs-CN-3, CPDs-CN-7, and CPDs-CN-10 were calculated as 9.5 × 106, 1.7 × 107, 9.3 × 107 and 2.3 × 107 s−1, respectively. Moreover, we recorded steady PL emission after excitation at λ = 350 nm (Fig. 6b), revealing that the spectrum of GCN exhibits a broad emission peak at 547 nm. With the incorporation of CPDs, the PL intensity decreased, which suggested that radiative recombination was significantly retarded [68]. The possibility of charge separation increased, while the radiative recombination of photogenerated charge carriers was suppressed by CPDs. This further confirms the role of CPDs as electron donors after photoexcitation for enhanced charge separation.

Electron chemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to further probe catalyst charge-transfer properties (Fig. 6c). The spectra of CPDs-CN featured smaller semicircles than that of GCN, indicating the lower charge-transfer resistance of CPDs-CN and, hence, their faster carrier transfer. Subsequently, time-dependent photoresponses were acquired using intermittent irradiation. According to Fig. 6d, the photocurrent density of CPDs-CN exceeded that of GCN, which strongly suggested that the charge generation properties of the former were superior to those of the latter. Taken together, our findings show that CPDs effectively broaden the light absorption region and promote photogenerated charge transfer and separation. CPDs were proved to extend the light-responsive range, contritubute to redistribute of photoinduced electron-hole pairs and thus enhanced the electron density of g-C3N4 [59]. The generation of reactive oxygen species by CPDs-CN-7 under light irradiation was investigated using sodium terephthalate as a probe [69]. After 10-h exposure to simulated sunlight, no characteristic fluorescence emission of 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid at λ = 430 nm[16] was detected (Fig. S29), which indicated the absence of photoinduced OH• generation. Thus, the photogenerated electrons and holes were concluded to play critical roles in H2 generation and substrate oxidation. Bearing the above in mind, the enhanced photocatalytic H2 production activity was conducive to the photo-conversion of hydrolysate-EG of PET via its alkaline hydrolysis, which further promoted the hydrolysis reaction. Thus, we efficiently coupled photocatalytic H2 generation and value-added chemical production with the upcycling of PET.

Herein, we have presented a route to upcycle PET plastic using CPDs-CN photocatalysts. Alkaline PET hydrolysis afforded high-purity EG and TPA. The photocatalysts undertake the conversion of EG into value-added chemicals, mainly including glycolic acid, glycolaldehyde, and ethanol, which provides electrons to reduce aqueous protons to generate H2. Under optimized conditions, an H2 production rate of 1034 ± 134 μmol g−1 h−1 was achieved. A TPA yield (304.7 ± 17.2 μmol) was almost twice that observed for the control group. TPA can be collected and therefore reused to realize the chemical recycling of plastic waste. The system provides a novel platform to produce the valuable organic chemicals and H2 fuel simultaneously, and the products are also spontaneously separated in the solid, solution and gaseous phase, respectively. The study on mechanism revealed that the introduction of CPDs noticeably extended the light-responsive range of GCN, favored the redistribution of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and markedly minimized the energy-wasteful recombination of these pairs to enhance photoactivity. Notably, the strongly alkaline residual could be fully utilized to avoid detrimental effects on the natural environment. This approach advances the global sustainability goals by combining the solar-powered production of H2 fuels with reclaimed plastics into value-added chemicals. Ultimately, we demonstrate a strategy that allows plastic waste to be used as a valuable feedstock and incentivizes PET reclamation to reduce plastic pollution.

Mei Han: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Shoujun Zhu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Chunlei Xia: Software, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Bai Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
